integrations_researched.name: Drizzle ORM

api_patterns.initialization_with_client:
const sql = neon(process.env.DATABASE_URL!);
const db = drizzle({ client: sql });

query_examples.upsert: await db.insert(users).values({ id: 1, name: 'Dan' }).onConflictDoUpdate({ target: users.id, set: { name: 'John' } });

http_driver.use_case: HTTP-based queries, lower latency for simple queries

serverless_driver.use_case: WebSocket-based connections, better for complex operations
serverless_driver.nodejs_requirement: Requires 'ws' package for Node.js v21 and earlier

gotchas: Drizzle has multiple drivers for Neon: neon-http (HTTP-based) and neon-serverless (WebSocket-based)
For Node.js v21 and earlier, neon-serverless requires installing 'ws' package and configuring neonConfig.webSocketConstructor
Use .returning() on insert/update/delete to get affected rows in PostgreSQL
Connection string must include proper authentication and SSL settings for Neon
Schema definitions must be created separately and imported
Type inference works via typeof table.$inferSelect and typeof table.$inferInsert

integrations_researched.name: Neon PostgreSQL Serverless Driver

usage_patterns.basic_query: const rows = await sql`SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id = ${postId}`;
usage_patterns.with_drizzle:
const sql = neon(process.env.DATABASE_URL!);
const db = drizzle({ client: sql });

global_config_options: neonConfig.pipelineConnect - Pipeline startup messages for performance
neonConfig.poolQueryViaFetch - Route Pool.query() over HTTP for lower latency

gotchas: Node.js v21 and earlier require explicit WebSocket constructor configuration
Must install 'ws' package separately for Node.js environments
Neon uses pooled connections by default - no manual pooling needed
Connection strings are specific to Neon endpoints (ep-*.*.aws.neon.tech format)
For edge/serverless environments, HTTP driver may have lower latency than WebSocket
WebSocket connections may timeout on long-running operations - use HTTP for simple queries

integrations_researched.name: Apollo Server

api_patterns.initialization: const server = new ApolloServer({ typeDefs, resolvers });
api_patterns.server_start: const { url } = await startStandaloneServer(server, { context: async () => ({ db }) });

database_access_via_context.description: Pass database connection through context to all resolvers

resolver_arguments.parent: Result from parent resolver (for nested fields)

best_practices: Access database through contextValue.db for consistency
Initialize database connection in context function
Use TypeScript types for type-safe resolvers
Handle errors properly and throw GraphQLError when needed

required_setup: context - Function returning context object with db connection

gotchas: Must install 'graphql' package separately (peer dependency)
Context function is called on EVERY request - ensure database connections are reused
Resolvers should not modify contextValue - treat it as read-only
Default resolvers exist for simple fields - only define custom resolvers when needed
Resolver chains: parent resolver result becomes 'parent' argument of child resolver
Type definitions and resolvers must match exactly or Apollo will throw errors
For production, consider using connection pooling in context instead of creating new connections

integrations_researched.name: GraphQL

verified_package.name: graphql
verified_package.note: Required peer dependency for Apollo Server

gotchas: graphql package is a peer dependency for most GraphQL servers
Version compatibility: ensure graphql version matches Apollo Server requirements
Schema-first approach: type definitions must be defined before resolvers

recommended_setup.description: Best practice for connecting GraphQL resolvers to Neon PostgreSQL via Drizzle ORM
steps: 2. Configure DATABASE_URL environment variable with Neon connection string
3. Create Drizzle schema definitions for all database tables
4. Initialize Drizzle client with Neon driver in db.ts module
5. Pass db instance through Apollo Server context
6. Access db from contextValue in all resolvers
7. Use Drizzle query methods (select, insert, update, delete) in resolvers

connection_pooling.description: Neon serverless driver handles connection pooling automatically
recommendations: Create single db instance and reuse across all resolvers
Pass db through context rather than creating new instances
Neon's serverless architecture handles scaling automatically
For high-traffic scenarios, consider neon-http driver for lower latency

error_handling.description: Proper error handling for database operations in resolvers

recommendations: Use drizzle-orm/neon-http driver for simple queries (lower latency via HTTP)
Create a centralized db module that exports configured Drizzle instance
Define TypeScript types for all resolvers using generated types from schema
Implement proper error handling in all database operations
Use .returning() on mutations to return inserted/updated data efficiently
Consider creating helper functions for common query patterns to reduce duplication across 50+ resolvers
Use Drizzle's query builder methods (eq, and, or) instead of raw SQL for type safety
Test database connections in development before deploying to production
Monitor Neon connection limits and configure appropriate timeouts
critical_implementation_notes: All 50+ resolvers must access database through contextValue.db for consistency
Drizzle schema definitions must exist for all tables before implementing resolvers
Environment variable DATABASE_URL must be set with valid Neon connection string
For Node.js environments with neon-serverless driver, install and configure 'ws' package
Maintain existing GraphQL API contracts - resolvers must return data matching schema types
Implement error handling from the start - database failures should throw GraphQLError
Connection pooling is automatic with Neon - do not create multiple db instances
Use .returning() on all mutations to avoid additional SELECT queries
next_steps_for_architect: Inventory all 50+ resolver locations across codebase
Verify Drizzle schema definitions exist for all data types used in resolvers
Identify which resolvers are queries vs mutations
Map each resolver to its corresponding database table
Plan migration strategy: update resolvers incrementally or all at once
Design error handling strategy for database failures
Create reusable query helper functions for common patterns
